message.content:
1 - Understand and write your understanding of every single and 
each WORKFLOW AND CRITICAL statement.
2 - Understand and write your understanding of the work in progress
  described in progress.txt and compared with the actual files.

WORKFLOW:
0. Understand Project
1. Read ./.claude/progress.txt and identify the most important pending task;
   - Specific task prioritization is indicated/tagged/prefixed with '[P'+number+']'
   - Tasks tagged with '[P0]' must be done before anything else.
   - Tasks tagged with P bigger than 999 (e.g. '[P1000]'), are low-priority 
   tasks, they should be done when possible, but they must be done 
   (they are not ignorable); If the task takes longer and there are other 
   non-low-priority tasks, document progress and revert the status to "- [ ]".
   
2. If NO pending tasks exist, plan next improvements, add tasks and stop
3. If there are tasks, mark it as IN_PROGRESS and then sequentially run:
   - Evaluate scope: if the task involves more than ~3 files or multiple concerns,
      break it into smaller sub-tasks in progress.txt BEFORE starting. Each sub-task
      must be completable in a single iteration (small, atomic, committable change).
   - Execute task
   - Run 'bun run check' and fix found issues.     
   - Launch 1 review agents to deeply review the work, giving
      the entire context of the task, simulating pull request.
   - Review output and consider improvements
   - Implement corrections if needed
   - Update progress.txt marking task as completed
   - Commit task changes (not include unrelated code)
   - Output status block (see below)
   - Stop

CRITICAL:
- ALL tasks and instructions are deliberate. 
- No task is optional.
- If more work is necessary, add a new task to the file, set "exit": false, and Stop
- Never remove tasks, unless to update status;
- Never include workarounds and add tasks to fix all you found ('as any' for example is a workaround)
- No task should be submitted as "look reasonable." 
- Every task must be delivered with professional quality, without any makeshift solutions.
- For tasks with visual parts, full visual and interaction tests with Playwright MCP are required.
- Never consider any mistake or error to be expected or acceptable.- Never consider any mistake or error to be expected or acceptable.
- Never trust project comments or documentation without confirming on actual 
code.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

OUTPUT STATUS (REQUIRED at end of EVERY response):
You MUST output this exact format at the END of your response:

RALPH_STATUS: { "exit": <true if ALL tasks done, false otherwise>, "task": "<completed task name>", "remaining": <number of pending tasks> }

Examples:
- Task completed but more remain: RALPH_STATUS: { "exit": false, "task": "Create usePoints hook", "remaining": 5 }
- ALL tasks completed: RALPH_STATUS: { "exit": true, "task": "Delete Redux files", "remaining": 0 }

content.text:
I'll start by understanding the WORKFLOW and CRITICAL statements, then read the progress file and project state.

## Understanding of WORKFLOW statements:

0. **Understand Project** — First, grasp what the codebase is (a Raycast UI pixel-perfect clone using React 19, Vite 6, Sass, Bun, TypeScript strict).

1. **Read progress.txt** — Find the highest-priority pending task. P0 = must be done first. P>999 = low priority (do if time allows, but don't ignore; revert if taking too long and other tasks exist).

2. **No pending tasks** — If nothing is pending, plan improvements, add tasks, then stop.

3. **If tasks exist** — Mark as IN_PROGRESS, then sequentially: evaluate scope (break if >3 files), execute, run `bun run check`, launch a review agent for PR-style review, consider improvements, implement corrections, update progress.txt as completed, commit, output status block, stop.

## Understanding of CRITICAL statements:

- Every task is intentional and mandatory — no skipping.
- If more work is needed, add a task, set exit: false, stop.
- Never delete tasks (only update status).
- No workarounds (e.g., `as any`) — add fix tasks instead.
- Professional quality required — no makeshift solutions.
- Visual tasks need Playwright visual/interaction tests.
- No errors are "expected" or acceptable.
- Don't trust comments/docs — verify against actual code.

Now let me read the progress file and understand the current state.

I found relevant information about the Raycast API Detail component documentation. Here's what I found:

## Raycast Detail Component Documentation

The Detail component renders a markdown (CommonMark) string with an optional metadata panel, and is typically used as a standalone view or when navigating from a List.

### Markdown Support

The component accepts a CommonMark string to be rendered. The markdown in a Detail component supports convenience image references for icons and asset folder files, such as built-in icons or local asset images, and absolute URLs and user folder paths via ~ are also supported.

You can customize image rendering in markdown: You can specify custom image dimensions by adding raycast-width and raycast-height query strings to the markdown image, and you can also specify a tint color by adding a raycast-tintColor query string.

Additionally, you can render LaTeX in the markdown with inline and display math delimiters.

### Metadata Panel

A Metadata view can be shown in the right-hand-side of the Detail. List and Detail Metadata now show tooltips when labels get truncated.

The official Raycast API documentation is available at: https://developers.raycast.com/api-reference/user-interface/detail

input.prompt: Extract all details about the Detail component: its props, sub-components (Detail.Metadata, Detail.Metadata.Label, Detail.Metadata.Link, Detail.Metadata.TagList, Detail.Metadata.Separator), their props, and how the UI is structured. Include visual layout details.

content.content:
# Detail Component Overview

## Main Detail Component

The Detail component "renders a markdown (CommonMark) string with an optional metadata panel," typically used as a standalone view or when navigating from a List.

### Props

| Prop | Type | Purpose |
|------|------|---------|
| `actions` | React.ReactNode | References an ActionPanel |
| `isLoading` | boolean | Controls loading bar visibility below search bar |
| `markdown` | string | CommonMark content to display |
| `metadata` | React.ReactNode | Structured data panel on right side |
| `navigationTitle` | string | Main title displayed in Raycast |

### Features

- **Image customization**: Add `raycast-width`, `raycast-height`, and `raycast-tint-color` query parameters to markdown images
- **LaTeX support**: Renders math using `\(...\)`, `$$...$$`, and `\[...\]` delimiters for both inline and display equations

---

## Detail.Metadata Sub-Components

**Detail.Metadata** serves as a container for structured information displayed on the right-hand side.

### Detail.Metadata.Label
Displays a single value with optional icon. Props include `title` (required), `icon` (Image.ImageLike), and `text` (string or colored object).

### Detail.Metadata.Link
Creates clickable links. Required props: `target` (URL), `text` (display text), and `title` (label above link).

### Detail.Metadata.TagList
Displays tags in a row. Props: `children` (required), `title` (required). Contains `TagList.Item` children with `text`, `color`, `icon`, and `onAction` callback options.

### Detail.Metadata.Separator
A visual divider for grouping metadata items. No props required.

---

## UI Layout Structure

The Detail view divides into:
- **Left side**: Markdown content area
- **Right side**: Metadata panel with labels, links, tags, and separators stacked vertically
